partI.instructions: Choose the single best answer for each question.

questions.stem: Which statements about computing innovations are correct? I. It includes a program as an integral part of its function. II. It must be a physical object users can touch. III. A concept like ride-matching, with no physical form, can be one.
options: I and II only
I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: I restates CRD-1.A.1: a computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function. III matches CRD-1.A.2: an innovation can be a nonphysical concept such as e-commerce or ride-matching. II is false — innovations may be physical, nonphysical software, or nonphysical concepts. A includes false II and drops true III. C includes false II and drops true I. D includes false II.

questions.stem: Four teammates disagree over whether a voting page should show artist names. Each states what it would take to accept the other design, and they settle on a version everyone supports. Which skills does this best illustrate?
options: Divide-and-conquer scheduling and fully independent work
Storyboarding and user-interface layout diagrams
Rapid prototyping followed by beta testing
Negotiation, conflict resolution, and consensus building
questions.rationale: CRD-1.C.1 lists communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation; stating acceptance conditions is negotiation, resolving the split is conflict resolution, and agreeing on one supported version is consensus building. A avoids the disagreement instead of resolving it and is not an interpersonal skill. B names design-phase activities (CRD-2.F.7). C names development phases, not the skills used in the discussion.

questions.stem: Two programmers use one live shared editor: one types while the other reviews each line, swapping roles. Which statement best describes how this supports collaboration?
options: It combines an online collaboration tool with pair programming, letting them share and give feedback on code in real time
It removes any need for documentation, since both watch the code as it is written
It guarantees the finished program will have no syntax errors before it runs
It replaces the testing phase, since reviewing code equals running test inputs
questions.rationale: CRD-1.B.1 says online tools let programmers share and give feedback on ideas and documents, and CRD-1.B.2 names pair programming — the shared editor with a driver and a live reviewer is both at once. B is wrong; documentation is still written throughout development (CRD-2.G.3). C is wrong; collaboration reduces but does not guarantee against errors, which testing and debugging find (CRD-2.I.5). D is wrong; testing runs defined inputs (CRD-2.J.1) — reading code is not a substitute.

questions.stem: A museum kiosk sits at a menu until a visitor taps an exhibit icon; the tap immediately starts a video regardless of what the kiosk was doing. Which statement best describes how this program operates?
options: It runs strictly top to bottom, so the video plays only after every menu statement finishes
The tap is output, since it changes what appears on the screen
The tap is an event supplying input, and event-driven statements execute when triggered, not in sequence
It can accept input only at startup, so each tap restarts the whole program
questions.rationale: CRD-2.C.2 says an event supplies input to a program, and CRD-2.C.5 says event-driven statements execute when triggered rather than in sequential flow — the tap is the trigger. A describes sequential flow, which the interrupt contradicts. B reverses the direction; the tap is input (CRD-2.C.1), the video is output. D is wrong; events can occur any time an action happens (CRD-2.C.3), not only at start.

questions.stem: Two readers give different responses for identical card taps. Which statement best describes how a program's output is produced?
options: Output is usually based on input and prior state, so the cards' different stored balances give different outputs
Output is unrelated to input, so the different responses are basically random
The readers must run unrelated programs, since one program cannot give two outputs
The second reader hit a syntax error triggered by the low card balance
questions.rationale: CRD-2.D.2 says output is usually based on a program's input or prior state, and CRD-2.C.4 says inputs usually affect output — the same program reads different balances and outputs differently. B contradicts CRD-2.C.4. C is wrong; a program must work for a variety of inputs (CRD-2.B.3), giving different outputs by design. D is wrong; a syntax error breaks the language's rules (CRD-2.I.2), not caused by the data a running program reads.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a program and a code segment?
options: A code segment is the hardware where a program's statements are stored
The terms are interchangeable; either can name an entire piece of software
A code segment is a complete program, while a program is a fragment that cannot run
A program performs a task when run; a code segment is statements that are part of a program
questions.rationale: CRD-2.B.1 defines a program (software) as statements that perform a task when run; CRD-2.B.2 defines a code segment as statements that are part of a program. A is wrong; both are statements, not hardware. B is wrong; the terms are not interchangeable — the segment is a part. C reverses them: the program is the whole, the segment the part.

questions.stem: A team is asked to 'help the food pantry.' Before designing anything, they interview staff and observe clients, and discover donated items expire unrecorded. Why was this early work valuable?
options: It let them skip design and prototyping and jump straight to the final version
Understanding the purpose improves the ability to develop it, and investigation reveals constraints and users' concerns
It guaranteed no testing phase would be needed, since users were already consulted
It removed the need for requirements, since observing users replaces a specification
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.A.2 + EK CRD-2.F.2 + EK CRD-2.F.3
questions.rationale: CRD-2.A.2 says understanding an innovation's purpose improves the ability to develop it, and CRD-2.F.2 says investigation identifies constraints and the concerns of users; interviews and observations are named methods (CRD-2.F.3). A is wrong; investigation informs design, it does not replace it (CRD-2.E.2). C is wrong; testing with defined inputs is still required (CRD-2.J.1). D is wrong; investigation produces the understanding used to write requirements (CRD-2.F.5), not a replacement for them.

questions.stem: A team verifies each feature before the next and returns to design after user feedback. Which terms best describe this process?
options: Ordered and exploratory, since they wrote everything before testing
Event-driven and modular, since each feature responds to a user action
Incremental and iterative — pieces verified one at a time, and feedback sends them back to an earlier phase
Random and trial-based, since revisiting a phase shows there was no plan
questions.rationale: CRD-2.E.4 defines incremental as breaking the problem into pieces verified before combining (matching before notifications), and CRD-2.E.3 defines iterative as revision from feedback that may revisit earlier phases (back to design). A is wrong; they tested piece by piece, and ordered versus exploratory describes planning (CRD-2.E.1). B is wrong; event-driven describes execution (CRD-2.C.5), not a process. D is wrong; revisiting phases is a feature of a deliberate iterative process.

questions.stem: A design note reads: 'When a gardener selects an open plot, the program must show a confirmation and message the coordinator.' Which term names this kind of statement?
options: A logic error report, since it describes behavior not yet coded
Documentation, since it explains how a finished segment was built
A program input, since it is text users send the program to process
A program requirement, since it describes how the program functions, including a user interaction
questions.rationale: CRD-2.F.4 says requirements describe how a program functions and may include user interactions; CRD-2.F.5 says the specification defines them. A is wrong; a logic error is a mistake in existing code (CRD-2.I.1), not planned behavior. B is wrong; documentation describes built code (CRD-2.G.1), not prescribed future behavior. C is wrong; inputs are data processed at run time (CRD-2.C.1), not a plan.

questions.stem: Midway through a project the lead developer leaves. A replacement becomes productive within days, finding the purpose and history of each procedure alone. Which practice best explains the smooth handoff?
options: The team wrote no comments, keeping the code shorter and faster to read
The team documented throughout development, which helps build and maintain correct programs when collaborating
The team planned to write all documentation in one session after release
The team relied on the computer to infer each procedure's purpose when run
questions.rationale: CRD-2.G.3 says document throughout development, and CRD-2.G.4 says documentation helps build and maintain correct programs individually or collaboratively — exactly what orients a newcomer. A is wrong; comments are for people and do not affect execution (CRD-2.G.2), so removing them only destroys the record. C is wrong; documentation deferred to the end would not exist midway. D is wrong; documentation is written by people (CRD-2.G.1), not generated by running the program.

questions.stem: A student's maze game uses a collision procedure written by a classmate and a color function from an open-source project. What must the program documentation include?
options: The origin or original author's name for each segment written by someone else
A note for the open-source function only, since a classmate's code needs no credit
Rewritten versions of both segments, since others' code may never appear in the program
A count of the lines the student personally wrote, to show the original percentage
questions.rationale: CRD-2.H.1 says acknowledge code developed collaboratively or from another source, and CRD-2.H.2 says the acknowledgement can appear in documentation and should give the origin or original author's name. B is wrong; CRD-2.H.1 covers collaboratively developed code too. C is wrong; borrowed code is fine when acknowledged. D is wrong; attribution names the source, not a line count.

questions.stem: A team codes a checkout program with no specification and no plan for evaluating it. Later, no one can say whether any output is wrong. Which design-phase step was most directly skipped?
options: Diagramming the layouts of the user-interface screens
Organizing the program's code into separate modules and functional components
Developing a testing strategy, since requirements are needed to choose inputs and expected outcomes
Brainstorming alternative solutions before choosing a design
questions.rationale: CRD-2.F.7 places developing a testing strategy in the design phase, and CRD-2.J.3 says requirements are needed to identify appropriate defined inputs; with no specification or plan, there are no expected outcomes to compare against. A and B — interface diagrams and modularity — affect layout and code organization, not whether outputs can be judged. D — brainstorming — widens the options considered but is not the missing piece; the described failure is the absence of requirements-based expected outcomes.

questions.stem: A badge tracker runs with no error messages but marks a badge complete after only four of five activities. The team fixes the comparison, then returns to an earlier phase to re-verify. Which classification and response fit?
options: A syntax error; they should have waited for the language to flag the line
A run-time error, since every mistake eventually halts with a message
An overflow error, since the activity count exceeded the storable range
A logic error — it runs but behaves incorrectly — and revisiting phases after testing is iterative
questions.rationale: CRD-2.I.1: a logic error is a mistake causing incorrect behavior — the tracker runs cleanly but gives wrong results. Returning to earlier phases after testing matches the iterative process (CRD-2.E.3). A is wrong; a syntax error breaks the language's rules and stops the program (CRD-2.I.2), but this runs. B is wrong; run-time errors halt during execution (CRD-2.I.3) — logic errors are silent. C is wrong; overflow involves a number outside the defined range (CRD-2.I.4), not a count of four.

questions.stem: A team classifies three bugs. I. A missing brace breaks the language's rules, so it will not run — labeled syntax error. II. The program runs but computes totals wrong — labeled logic error. III. A counter is set outside its storable range — labeled overflow error. Which labels are correct?
options: I, II, and III
I and II only
II and III only
I and III only
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.I.1 + EK CRD-2.I.2 + EK CRD-2.I.4
questions.rationale: All three match the taxonomy: a broken language rule is a syntax error (CRD-2.I.2); running but computing wrong is a logic error (CRD-2.I.1); a number outside the defined range is an overflow error (CRD-2.I.4). B wrongly rejects III. C wrongly rejects I. D wrongly rejects II.

questions.stem: A lap counter has a maximum value of 65,535. The test plan uses 10, 500, and 1,000, and all pass. Which change best addresses the risk this plan misses, and which error does it target?
options: Add mid-range values like 20,000 and 30,000, targeting a syntax error at those values
Add values at and just beyond the maximum, like 65,535 and 65,536, targeting an overflow error
Remove 10 from the plan, since small values give no useful information
Rerun the same three passing inputs several times, targeting a run-time error from repetition
questions.rationale: CRD-2.J.2 says test at or just beyond the extremes, and CRD-2.I.4 defines overflow as a number outside the defined range — testing 65,535 and 65,536 is the boundary probe the mid-range-only plan lacks. A is wrong; syntax errors break language rules (CRD-2.I.2), independent of input values. C is wrong; low extremes matter, and removing coverage weakens the plan. D is wrong; repeating passing inputs exercises nothing new — the risk lies past the maximum.

partII.instructions: Answer in complete sentences; use precise development-process, documentation, and error vocabulary from Big Idea 1.

questions.prompt: A three-student team is building SeedSwap, an app that catalogs a community seed library and matches gardeners who want to trade seeds. The team begins by interviewing gardeners about how trades happen now, sketches the main screens on paper, releases a rough working version to five gardeners, and reworks the trade-matching screen twice based on their feedback. From the first week, the team writes comments describing what each procedure does and why it was written that way. The finished program also includes a QR-code-scanning procedure the team downloaded from an open-source project.
parts: (a) Using the phases commonly used when developing a program, explain how the team's process is iterative.
(b) Explain TWO ways the team's habit of documenting the program throughout development benefits the team.
(c) Describe what the team must do to properly acknowledge the QR-code-scanning procedure, including where the acknowledgment can appear and what it must contain.
sample: (a) The team's work moves through the commonly used development phases: interviewing gardeners is investigating and reflecting, sketching screens is designing, releasing a rough working version is prototyping, and giving it to five gardeners to use is testing (CRD-2.E.2). The process is iterative because the team refines and revises based on feedback: after gardeners tested the prototype, the team returned to the design phase and reworked the trade-matching screen twice. Revisiting earlier phases in response to feedback, testing, or reflection is exactly what makes a development process iterative (CRD-2.E.3).

(b) First, documentation written throughout development helps the team develop and maintain a correct program while collaborating: any of the three students can read the comments to understand what a procedure does and why, instead of relying on the person who wrote it (CRD-2.G.4). Second, documenting as they go, rather than reconstructing explanations after release, follows the CED's guidance that programmers should document a program throughout its development (CRD-2.G.3); the comments record decisions while they are fresh, and since comments are written for people and do not affect how the program runs (CRD-2.G.2), there is no performance cost to keeping them.

(c) Because the QR-code-scanning procedure was written by another source, the team must acknowledge it (CRD-2.H.1). The acknowledgment can be placed in the program documentation, such as a comment near the procedure, and it must identify the origin of the code or the original author's name, for example naming the open-source project the procedure came from (CRD-2.H.2).
rubric: 2 pts: Part (a) names the commonly used phases (investigating and reflecting, designing, prototyping, testing) and maps at least three of them to the team's actions (CRD-2.E.2).

1 pt: Part (a) explains that the process is iterative: refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection, including revisiting earlier phases, shown by the twice-reworked matching screen (CRD-2.E.3).

1 pt: Part (b) shows understanding of what documentation is: a written description of the function of a code segment, event, procedure, or program and how it was developed; comments are read by people and do not affect execution (CRD-2.G.1, CRD-2.G.2).

2 pts: Part (b) gives two distinct benefits, such as developing and maintaining correct programs in a collaborative environment (CRD-2.G.4) and documenting throughout development rather than at the end (CRD-2.G.3). 1 pt per benefit.

2 pts: Part (c) states that borrowed code must be acknowledged (CRD-2.H.1), that the acknowledgment can appear in the program documentation, and that it must include the origin or original author's name (CRD-2.H.2).
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.E.2 + EK CRD-2.E.3 + EK CRD-2.G.1 + EK CRD-2.G.2 + EK CRD-2.G.3 + EK CRD-2.G.4 + EK CRD-2.H.1 + EK CRD-2.H.2

questions.prompt: RinkPass sells tickets for public ice-skating sessions. Its program requirements state that a booking must contain from 1 to 40 skaters and that groups of 6 or more receive a group discount. Three problems are reported: (1) after an edit, the program will not run at all, and the editor points to a line with a missing closing parenthesis; (2) the program runs, but a group of exactly 6 skaters is charged full price instead of the group rate; (3) the running program halts with an error message when a booking form is submitted with the skater-count field left empty.
parts: (a) Classify each of the three reported problems by error type, and justify each classification.
(b) Using the requirements, describe a set of defined test inputs for the skater count that tests values at and just beyond the extremes, and state the expected outcome for each input.
(c) Describe TWO effective methods the team could use to find and correct problem 2, and explain how each method would reveal the error.
sample: (a) Problem 1 is a syntax error: a missing closing parenthesis means the rules of the programming language are not followed, which is why the program cannot run (CRD-2.I.2). Problem 2 is a logic error: the program runs without complaint but behaves incorrectly, charging a 6-skater group full price when the requirements say 6 or more get the discount, so the mistake is in the algorithm's comparison (CRD-2.I.1). Problem 3 is a run-time error: the mistake appears during the execution of the program, which halts with an error message when it tries to process the empty field (CRD-2.I.3).

(b) The requirements say bookings hold 1 to 40 skaters, and program requirements are what identify appropriate defined inputs (CRD-2.J.3). Test inputs should demonstrate the different expected outcomes at or just beyond the extremes (CRD-2.J.2): 0 skaters (just beyond the minimum, expected outcome: booking rejected), 1 skater (at the minimum, expected: accepted at full price), 40 skaters (at the maximum, expected: accepted with the group discount), and 41 skaters (just beyond the maximum, expected: rejected). Because the discount boundary is also part of the requirements, 5 skaters (expected: full price) and 6 skaters (expected: group rate) should be tested too. The results from these tests are then used to revise the program (CRD-2.J.1).

(c) One effective method is running test cases at the discount boundary: testing 5, 6, and 7 skaters against the expected prices shows immediately that 6 produces the wrong outcome, isolating the faulty comparison (CRD-2.I.5, CRD-2.J.1). A second method is hand tracing the pricing code with the value 6: stepping through each statement and writing down variable values reveals whether the comparison uses 'greater than 6' where the requirements demand 'greater than or equal to 6' (CRD-2.I.5). Adding extra output statements that display the skater count and which price branch executed, or stepping through with a debugger, would expose the same faulty branch.
rubric: 3 pts: Part (a) correctly classifies and justifies all three: syntax error, the language's rules are not followed (CRD-2.I.2); logic error, the program runs but behaves incorrectly (CRD-2.I.1); run-time error, the mistake occurs during program execution (CRD-2.I.3). 1 pt per correct classification with justification.

2 pts: Part (b) chooses inputs at and just beyond the extremes of the 1-to-40 requirement, such as 0, 1, 40, and 41 (CRD-2.J.2), and notes that the requirements are what identify appropriate defined inputs (CRD-2.J.3).
1 pt: Part (b) states an expected outcome for each defined input and indicates that test results are used to revise the program (CRD-2.J.1).
2 pts: Part (c) names two distinct methods from CRD-2.I.5 (test cases, hand tracing, visualizations, debuggers, adding extra output statements). 1 pt per method.
1 pt: Part (c) explains how at least one chosen method exposes the faulty boundary comparison at 6 skaters (e.g., hand tracing the comparison with the value 6, or a boundary test case at 5/6/7).
questions.ek: EK CRD-2.I.1 + EK CRD-2.I.2 + EK CRD-2.I.3 + EK CRD-2.I.5 + EK CRD-2.J.1 + EK CRD-2.J.2 + EK CRD-2.J.3
